title: The Upgrade Journey Exists - Solstice Just Doesn't Sell It
subtitle: Solstice Espresso · category whitespace & merchandising analysis · public-data mode
why_it_matters: Solstice's accessory catalog is 45% cleaning supplies and spare parts, with exactly one personalization item - sold on an alphabetized parts page. Its premium competitor runs a storefront built entirely around the owner's upgrade journey, on a catalog half Solstice's commodity weight. This analysis runs on catalog and storefront data alone; the close is about getting the one dataset that would size the prize.

diagnosis.verdict: A merchandising void, not a catalog void - pending behavioral confirmation.
diagnosis.narrative: Public data can't see attach, spend, mix, or channel, so the diagnosis rests on what can be audited: what's offered and how it's sold. The catalog already contains a credible enthusiast path - precision scale, bottomless portafilter, walnut tamper handle, shot timer - presented as an undifferentiated parts list, with nothing offered at machine checkout. The cheapest consistent hypothesis is that Solstice under-monetizes owners it already has. Attach, spend, and mix data (as buyer_behavior.yaml) would confirm it and upgrade this analysis automatically.

composition.headline: One personalization SKU against a four-item program
composition.narrative: Offer-side view: Solstice's catalog is 45% consumables and maintenance against Barista Culture's 21%, and 5% visible personalization against 29% - one walnut tamper handle versus a program of handles, knobs, and panels with a bespoke finish option behind it. Modena shows the other failure mode: the deepest parts catalog and no guidance at all.

benchmark_note.headline: Benchmark the selling behavior, not the shelf list
benchmark_note.narrative: Without purchase data we can't prove Barista Culture's buyers buy personalization - so the benchmark is the strongest observable proxy, labeled as such: sustained merchandising investment. Nobody keeps funding three named kits, a bespoke wood program, and an owner-journey storefront for categories that don't convert. Modena is the caution against presence-benchmarking: it offers precision gear too, and merchandises none of it.

storefront_critique.headline: The catalog has an enthusiast story; the storefront tells none of it
bullets: One alphabetized "Parts & Accessories" page; the only navigation aid is a machine-compatibility filter.
The walnut tamper handle - the single item that could anchor an upgrade story - sits between water filters and cleaning cloths.
A new Lumen owner checking out sees no kit, no "what you'll want in week one," no styled counter photo.
No bundles, no named kits, no bespoke option anywhere.

merchandising_benchmark.headline: Kits at checkout vs a parts counter
merchandising_benchmark.narrative: Barista Culture sells the journey - three named kits offered at machine checkout, an owner-journey storefront, made-to-order wood in four finishes. Modena documents fitment beautifully and guides nobody.

packs.headline: Three kits - hypotheses from the existing catalog
packs.narrative: Public-data mode: these are merchandising hypotheses to test, not sized opportunities. Every SKU already exists; each kit maps to an owner moment. No affinity data exists, so no lifestyle theming beyond the product's own ritual is proposed.

items.name: First Morning Kit
items.target: the new Lumen/Meridiana owner, at machine checkout

items.name: Counter Piece Kit
items.target: pride of ownership - the dressed bar

pricing_note.headline: Price is not the lever
pricing_note.narrative: On confirmed like-for-like items Solstice is at or below peers: bottomless portafilter $79 vs $85 (−7%); precision basket $32 vs $29 (+10% - a $3 gap on a $30 item). The personalization assortment void and the absent merchandising layer are the levers. No pricing action recommended.

now.action: Launch the three kits at bundle pricing
now.detail: First Morning Kit offered at machine checkout - the moment of highest intent.
Owner-journey sections replacing the alphabetical list; a styled counter photo on every machine page; the walnut handle surfaced as the hero of an upgrade story.

next.action: Build the personalization range the void demands
next.detail: Panels or accent kits and a second wood finish - following Barista Culture's bespoke playbook only if kit take-rate supports it.
Flow control for the Pro journey.

next_step.headline: One dataset changes everything
next_step.narrative: Pull attach rate, median accessory spend, and purchase mix from store analytics; drop it in as buyer_behavior.yaml and re-run. Until then: ship the First Morning Kit and measure checkout take-rate for 60 days.

mapping_resolutions.reason: workflow measurement tool, not an extraction-path component

comparability_calls: Knock Box Compact ($39) vs Barista Culture Knock Box Large ($59) - different sizes, used directionally only
Walnut Tamper Handle ($49) vs Walnut Handle & Knob Set ($129) - rejected: different scope
data_flags: All data in this fixture is synthetic demonstration data; brands and products are fictional.
No buyer-behavior data supplied - every percentage in this deck is catalog share (offer-side); no behavioral claims are made.
Merchandising attributes come from a simulated storefront audit (July 2026).
appendix.figure_types_note: Catalog share = share of SKUs offered (offer-side, not behavioral) - the only figure type public-data mode can support. Sections labeled 'proxy' stand in for unobserved purchase behavior.
